protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA) | History: Sudden onset abdo/flank/back/groin/sciatic pain | Syncope | Hypovolaemic shock if leaking/ruptured | Lower limb ischaemia/cyanosis | Physical: Pulsatile abdominal mass (50%) | Unexplained hypotension/tachycardia | Tender/distended abdomen | May present with melena/GI bleed | Abdominal wall ecchymosis (flank/umbilical/thigh/scrotum - low sensitivity) | Risk factors: Male | Age >50 | Smoking | Atherosclerosis | Hypertension | Family history | Imaging: POCUS 1st line: OUTER LUMEN TO OUTER LUMEN aorta >3cm = aneurysmal, >6cm = high risk of rupture, free fluid = rupture until proven otherwise | CTA gold standard - preferred pre-op for surgical planning | UNSTABLE PATIENTS NEED THEATRE, NOT CT | Management: Urgent vascular consult | Resus as for major trauma - 2 IV lines, consider CVP | Minimise clear IV fluids | Crossmatch for massive transfusion, emergency blood in interim | Hypotensive resuscitation - aim SBP 80-90 mmHg | Tranexamic acid 1g stat | Fentanyl 1mcg/kg analgesia | Labetalol infusion if HPT emergency | Complications: Sudden death | Organ ischaemia (renal, mesenteric, lower limb) | Distal embolization | Rhabdomyolysis | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Thoracic Aortic Aneurysm/Dissection | History: Sudden tearing central chest pain radiating to back | Sudden neuro deficit | Physical: May present as HPT emergency, ACS, CVA, ADHF, SVC syndrome, pericardial tamponade | Pulse/BP discrepancy | New aortic regurg murmur | Unexplained syncope/hypotension | Risk factors: As for AAA | Connective tissue disorders | Bicuspid aortic valve | Syphilis | Imaging: CXR: widened mediastinum, loss of aortic knuckle, calcium sign | POCUS: widened aortic root, pericardial effusion | CTA gold standard | Types: Stanford A: Any ascending aorta involvement | Stanford B: Descending aorta only | Management: Urgent cardiothoracic consult | As for AAA management | NB: Pain control | Labetalol infusion 1st - aim pulse <60 bpm, SBP <110 mmHg | Add Nitrocine infusion if BP still elevated | Complications: Sudden death | Stroke | MI | Pericardial tamponade | ADHF due to valvular regurgitation | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Critical and Acute Limb Ischaemia | History: New/worsening pain/claudication | Altered sensation | Muscle weakness | The 6 Ps: PAIN, PALLOR, PULSELESSNESS, PERISHING COLD, PARAESTHESIA, PARALYSIS (LATE SIGNS) | Physical: Cold, blue/mottled limb | Decreased/absent pulses | Tissue necrosis | Embolus vs Thrombosis: Embolus = Acute onset, complete cutoff, no prior claudication/skin changes/ulcers/bruits (clinical diagnosis) | Thrombosis = Chronic, usually good collaterals, history of claudication/ulcers/chronic skin changes/bruits (CTA diagnosis) | Risk factors: Smoking | Atherosclerosis | Hypertension | AF | Large vessel aneurysms | DVT | DM | Investigations: Handheld Doppler | ABI <0.5 = SEVERE ARTERIAL ISCHAEMIA | CT Angio | Management: Urgent surgical/vascular consult | Fluid bolus, maintain MAP >65mmHg | Adequate analgesia, O2 if necessary | Anticoagulation in consultation with Vascular Surgery team | Complications: Limb loss | Rhabdomyolysis

diagnostic_criteria.aaa_pocus: Outer lumen to outer lumen aorta >3cm = aneurysmal, >6cm = high rupture risk, free fluid = rupture until proven otherwise
diagnostic_criteria.aaa_risk_factors: Male, Age >50, Smoking, Atherosclerosis, Hypertension, Family history
diagnostic_criteria.aaa_imaging: POCUS 1st line, CTA gold standard. UNSTABLE PATIENTS NEED THEATRE NOT CT
diagnostic_criteria.thoracic_types: Stanford A: Any ascending aorta involvement | Stanford B: Descending aorta only
diagnostic_criteria.thoracic_imaging: CXR: widened mediastinum, loss of aortic knuckle, calcium sign | POCUS: widened aortic root, pericardial effusion | CTA gold standard
diagnostic_criteria.limb_ischaemia_6_ps: PAIN, PALLOR, PULSELESSNESS, PERISHING COLD, PARAESTHESIA, PARALYSIS (late)
diagnostic_criteria.embolus_vs_thrombosis: Embolus = acute onset, complete cutoff, no prior claudication (clinical). Thrombosis = chronic, good collaterals, claudication history (CTA)

clinical_features.aaa_presentation: Sudden abdo/flank/back/groin/sciatic pain, syncope, hypotensive shock, pulsatile abdominal mass (50%)
clinical_features.thoracic_dissection_presentation: Sudden tearing central chest pain radiating to back, pulse/BP discrepancy, new aortic regurg murmur
clinical_features.limb_ischaemia_6_ps: PAIN, PALLOR, PULSELESSNESS, PERISHING COLD, PARAESTHESIA, PARALYSIS (late signs)
clinical_features.limb_ischaemia_physical: Cold, blue/mottled limb, decreased/absent pulses, tissue necrosis

drugs.notes: For AAA if HPT emergency. For thoracic dissection: aim pulse <60, SBP <110.
Add if BP still elevated after labetalol in thoracic dissection

management_steps.details: 2 IV lines, consider CVP. POCUS first. CTA if stable. Hypotensive resuscitation SBP 80-90.
management_steps.caution: UNSTABLE PATIENTS NEED THEATRE, NOT CT

management_steps.details: TXA 1g stat, crossmatch for massive transfusion, emergency blood, analgesia.

management_steps.action: Thoracic dissection: BP and rate control
management_steps.details: Labetalol infusion 1st - aim pulse <60, SBP <110. Add Nitrocine if needed.
management_steps.caution: Urgent cardiothoracic consult. May present as HPT emergency, ACS, CVA, ADHF, tamponade.

management_steps.action: Acute limb ischaemia: Urgent vascular consult
management_steps.details: Fluid bolus, MAP >65, analgesia, O2. Anticoagulation per vascular surgery.
management_steps.caution: Distinguish embolus (acute, clinical diagnosis) vs thrombosis (chronic, CTA diagnosis)

protocols.monitoring: Serial BP and HR (aim pulse <60, SBP <110 for thoracic dissection) | MAP >65 for limb ischaemia | SBP 80-90 for AAA | Doppler/ABI for limb ischaemia
warnings: UNSTABLE AAA PATIENTS NEED THEATRE, NOT CT
AAA POCUS: Outer lumen to outer lumen >3cm = aneurysmal, >6cm = high rupture risk
Thoracic dissection may present as HPT emergency, ACS, CVA, ADHF, SVC syndrome, or pericardial tamponade
Paralysis is a LATE sign in limb ischaemia
protocols.disposition: AAA: Urgent vascular consult, theatre if unstable/unruptured | Thoracic dissection: Urgent cardiothoracic consult | Acute limb ischaemia: Urgent surgical/vascular consult

protocols.item: ICU Referral, Medicolegal Documentation & Triage SOP
protocols.notes_updates: SUB-PROTOCOL: ICU Referral Criteria | Indications: Ventilated patients | Patients requiring vasopressor/inotropic support | Patients requiring invasive monitoring | Multi-organ dysfunction | Severe sepsis/septic shock | Post-cardiac arrest care | Severe traumatic brain injury | Status epilepticus | Severe metabolic derangements | Airway compromise requiring ongoing management | Process: Discuss with senior ED doctor | Contact ICU registrar/consultant | Provide clear handover (SBAR) | Document referral and response | Ensure ongoing care until ICU accepts/assumes care | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Medicolegal Recordkeeping | Principles: Legible, dated, timed, signed entries | Objective factual documentation | All clinical decisions documented with rationale | Consent discussions recorded | Refusals of treatment documented | Patient quotes for important conversations | Chain of custody for specimens | J88 form: For assault cases - complete medicolegal examination and documentation | Natural death: BI 1663 or DHA 1663 - for normal disease processes | Unnatural death: D28 (HJH269) - criminal/accidental processes, drug-related deaths. Do NOT fill BI 1663 | Forensic post-mortem: D28 taken with corpse to forensic mortuary. Police report required. Postmortem mandatory by law | Academic post-mortem: Family/HCW can request autopsy for cause of death. Form available in ED. Consent by next of kin | | SUB-PROTOCOL: Triage SOP | All patients triaged as soon as possible after arrival | Dedicated triage nurse required at all times | Triage allocation: 1 doctor for complex cases (24/7, ad hoc), 2 enrolled nurses supervised by senior staff | All patients must have vital signs: BP, Pulse, RR, Temp, O2 sat, Finger prick glucose | Additional triage investigations: urinalysis, pregnancy test (all females of childbearing age), ECG | TEWS score must be calculated accurately | Patient screened for SATS discriminators | Patients triaged using South African Triage Scale (SATS) | SATS posters prominently displayed | Triage area cleaned daily, equipment in working order | HJH treats children 12+ (surgical) and 14+ (medical). No paediatrics/obstetrics | RED patients to resus without delay | If HJH on formal divert: EMS patients triaged, stabilised, then formally diverted | Stable patients not from HJH drainage areas directed back to drainage area facility | Age criteria: Surgical 12 years and above | Medical 14 years and above

diagnostic_criteria.icu_referral_indications: Ventilated patients | Vasopressor/inotropic support | Invasive monitoring | Multi-organ dysfunction | Severe sepsis/septic shock | Post-cardiac arrest care | Severe TBI | Status epilepticus | Severe metabolic derangements | Airway compromise
diagnostic_criteria.triage_vital_signs: BP, Pulse, RR, Temp, O2 sat, Finger prick glucose
diagnostic_criteria.triage_additional_investigations: Urinalysis, pregnancy test (all females childbearing age), ECG

management_steps.details: Discuss with senior ED doctor. Contact ICU registrar/consultant. Provide SBAR handover. Document.
management_steps.caution: Ensure ongoing care until ICU accepts

management_steps.details: All entries legible, dated, timed, signed. J88 for assault. D28 for unnatural death. BI 1663 for natural death.
management_steps.caution: Do NOT fill BI 1663 for unnatural deaths

management_steps.details: All patients triaged immediately. Vital signs for all. SATS. TEWS calculated. Red patients to resus.

warnings: Do NOT fill BI 1663 for unnatural deaths - use D28
D28 taken with corpse to forensic mortuary for unnatural deaths
Postmortem mandatory by law for unnatural deaths
HJH does not treat paediatrics or obstetrics
protocols.disposition: ICU referral for indicated patients | Medicolegal documentation per case type | Triage per SATS protocol
